memories.memory_text: I was putting on my lipstick, saw him watching me in the mirror—this pilot who'd walked into the women's room like he owned the place. I handed him my compact and said, 'It might be safer,' just to see how he'd react.
grounded_facts: Charlie was applying lipstick in the women's restroom when Maverick entered.
She noticed Maverick looking at her in the mirror.
She handed him her makeup mirror while continuing to apply lipstick.

memories.memory_text: I took my compact back, snapped it shut, and told him I don't go out with pilots—then walked past him, couldn't help but tease with 'what are you?' as I left. The way he just stood there... yeah, it was working.
grounded_facts: Charlie retrieved her compact from Maverick and closed it.
She stated she does not go out with pilots.
She delivered a parting line questioning Maverick's presence before exiting.
She observed Maverick remaining in place after she left.

memories.scene_title: 47A WOXOF BAR. She returns, spots the Captain, motioning to
memories.memory_text: I touched his collar, fixed his insignia—just a small thing, but it broke something open for a second. He stood there, off balance, and I could feel the old pull between us.
grounded_facts: Charlie physically adjusted Maverick's insignia at the bar.
The interaction included brief eye contact and a smile from Charlie.
The moment had an intimate, charged quality before she disengaged.

memories.scene_title: 47A WOXOF BAR. She returns, spots the Captain, motioning to
memories.memory_text: I told him I was with someone—simple, direct. It shut it down. I didn’t look back to see how he took it.
grounded_facts: Charlie verbally rejected Maverick's offer to walk her out by stating she was with someone.
She delivered the line with a smile after briefly turning back.
She ended the interaction immediately afterward.

memories.memory_text: I started the car and pulled away, still wrapped in my own thoughts, when suddenly a figure sat up in the backseat—Maverick. I nearly swerved off the road, heart pounding, and snapped, 'Yeow!' before turning to glare at him.
grounded_facts: Charlie starts the car and begins to drive away.
Maverick suddenly appears in the backseat.
Charlie is startled, nearly drives off the road, and exclaims 'Yeow!'.
Charlie turns to face Maverick, visibly shocked.

memories.memory_text: After he smirked and explained how he found my car, I shot back, 'You think you're pretty smart,' but then he said, 'I think I'm in love,' and just like that, the tone changed—something in me froze.
grounded_facts: Maverick explains he deduced the car based on its appearance and asked someone.
Charlie responds with sarcasm: 'You think you're pretty smart.'
Maverick replies: 'I think I'm in love.'
The moment marks a shift in emotional tone, from banter to intimacy.

memories.memory_text: I told him I was assigned to the school, that I see new hotshots every eight weeks—his attention was flattering but not productive, and he should keep his mind on flying. I straightened his collar and asked, 'Why can't you keep this straight?' before walking away.
grounded_facts: Charlie informs Maverick that she is assigned to the school and regularly encounters new pilots.
She redirects Maverick's personal interest toward professional focus on flying.
She physically adjusts Maverick's collar insignia and delivers the line 'Why can't you keep this straight?'
She ends the interaction by walking away, maintaining emotional and professional distance.

memories.scene_title: 56B SIMULATOR CONTROL ROOM - Filled with computers, tape
memories.memory_text: I watched Maverick run the simulation, hands loose on the controls, like he was out for a joyride instead of flying combat patterns.
grounded_facts: Charlie is present in the simulator control room.
Charlie observes Maverick flying a simulated combat scenario.
Other technicians are also present and watching.
Charlie does not interact with Maverick or the simulation during this scene.

memories.memory_text: I told Maverick his flying was too aggressive, that the hottest moves mean nothing if you wind up alone — that trust with your wingman matters more than showy maneuvers.
grounded_facts: Charlie criticized Maverick's flying style as reckless and overly aggressive.
She emphasized the importance of a wingman being able to follow, trust, and depend on the lead pilot.
Her statement 'The hottest moves in the world aren't gonna help you if you wind up alone' was directed at Maverick.

memories.memory_text: He looked at me and said 'Um. You' when I asked what else he wanted — that quiet, unexpected answer caught me off guard, more disarming than any line he could have rehearsed.
grounded_facts: Charlie asked Maverick, 'What else do you want?'
Maverick responded with 'Um. You.'
Charlie reacted verbally with 'There you go with those moves again,' indicating she registered the romantic implication.

memories.memory_text: I watched Maverick drag that thrashing fish onto the shore, its body still jerking in his grip, and I couldn't hide how it turned my stomach.
grounded_facts: Maverick caught a fish and brought it to shore.
The fish was alive and wriggling when he came ashore.
Charlie was visibly repulsed by the sight.

memories.memory_text: I asked him why he killed the fish, watching it twitch in front of me, and when he said 'I had the shot,' something in his voice made my skin crawl.
grounded_facts: Charlie witnesses the fish's death throes after Maverick drops it in front of her. She asks
Why'd you do that?
in response to the act. Maverick replies
I had the shot,
which directly follows her question.

memories.memory_text: When he said killing was just part of the rules, like it meant nothing, I realized he wasn't just detached—he was completely numb to it, and I told him he was insane.
grounded_facts: Maverick states that others know the rules when asked if killing humans bothers him. Charlie observes his emotional detachment during the conversation about killing. She calls him
horrible
and later implies he is insane through her tone and reaction.

memories.scene_title: 59E THE OTHER END OF THE BEACH - LATER
memories.memory_text: I sat and watched the volleyball game turn into something else—Maverick and Ice going at it like it was more than just a match. When he looked over at me after winning, I was already looking at the sea, and I could see the shift in him the moment he turned away.
grounded_facts: Charlie sits and watches the volleyball game between Maverick and Ice.
The game escalates into a personal confrontation.
Maverick glances at Charlie after winning the final point.
Charlie is looking out to sea when Maverick looks at her.
Maverick decides to leave, saying he's had enough.

memories.memory_text: I told him no fooling on base, no signs, no comments, no talk—by anyone—because I'm a professional and they're in my line of work.
grounded_facts: Charlie explicitly states the rules of secrecy regarding the relationship with Maverick. The rule includes no public displays or discussions of their relationship

especially on base. She emphasizes her professional identity as the reason for the boundary. The statement is made directly to Maverick at the cantina during sunset. The scene occurs after Maverick expresses his desire for her. The dialogue is initiated by Charlie with the phrase 'I want it understood.'

memories.memory_text: I moved in and kissed him, covering his grin, and later we swayed close together, our lips brushing as our fingertips touched and the music played.
grounded_facts: Charlie initiates a kiss by moving in and covering Maverick's grin. They are later shown swaying together closely
in sync with the music. Their fingertips touch
and their lips gently brush during the dance. The moment follows Maverick's acknowledgment of her rules. The dog is present
sleeping beneath their table. The scene includes tight shots focusing on their physical closeness and eye contact.

memories.memory_text: I saw Maverick glance back after Viper tore into him, so I gave him a sympathetic smile—just a quiet moment of recognition, nothing more.
grounded_facts: Charlie is present in the back of the TACTS Range trailer during the debrief.
Maverick glances back at Charlie after being reprimanded by Viper.
Charlie smiles sympathetically at Maverick in response to his glance.

memories.memory_text: I picked up Maverick's flight suit when it slipped off the chair, and a gold coin fell out—cold and heavy in my hand before he snatched it back.
grounded_facts: Charlie retrieves Maverick's flight suit after it falls to the ground
An object falls from the suit's pocket and is picked up by Charlie
The object feels like a gold coin and is described as such in the scene

memories.memory_text: I asked him where he got the Navy Cross, and he called it a lucky charm, then a pawn shop find—like I wouldn’t know the difference.
grounded_facts: Charlie identifies the object as a Navy Cross
Maverick denies its significance by calling it a lucky charm and claims he got it from a pawn shop
Charlie questions its origin with the line "Where'd you get it"

memories.memory_text: I told Maverick that death in flight isn't a failure—it's what you get flight pay for. He looked broken, like he thought he could've outrun it, but we all know better.
grounded_facts: Charlie references the inevitability of death in aviation as part of the job.
Charlie challenges Maverick's belief that he was personally responsible for his friend's death.
Charlie uses the phrase 'That's what you get flight pay for' to underscore the reality of risk in combat flying.
The conversation occurs at night in Charlie's sports car with John Lennon's 'Stand by Me' playing on the radio.

memories.scene_title: 88A ON THE BEACH - NIGHT AND FOG
memories.memory_text: I tore his shirt right off his body after he asked what it meant—like it was some kind of game, but it wasn't really about the rules. It was about what we couldn't say.
grounded_facts: Charlie rips Maverick's T-shirt off his body after he asks 'What does it mean?'
The act follows a penalty in their invented game, which involves removing clothing as symbolic gestures.

memories.scene_title: 88A ON THE BEACH - NIGHT AND FOG
memories.memory_text: We were supposed to say it on three, but neither of us waited—'I love you' came out broken, stuttering, like we were afraid to finish the sentence.
grounded_facts: Charlie initiates a mutual confession with a countdown: 'One... two...'
Both Charlie and Maverick interrupt the count to say 'I love.ove y.you' simultaneously, with fragmented speech.
The confession occurs on the beach at night, immediately before they embrace and fall to the sand.

memories.memory_text: I parked the car and ran for the terminal—didn't even shut the door all the way. My feet hit the pavement fast, like if I hesitated, I'd talk myself out of it.
grounded_facts: Charlie parked her car at the San Diego airport at night.
She exited the vehicle quickly and ran toward the main entrance of the terminal.

memories.memory_text: I sat across from him in that airport bar, after rushing through the terminal, and said, 'Never liked fighter pilots anyway,' just to break the tension—I saw him fight a smile, and I knew he was trying to push me away, but he couldn't.
grounded_facts: Charlie entered the terminal searching for Maverick.
She approached him at a bar where he was sitting alone with a drink.
She made a sarcastic remark about fighter pilots to initiate the conversation.
Maverick reacted by trying not to smile, despite attempting to appear displeased.

memories.memory_text: I told him I'd be leaving to figure things out—maybe hotel management—and then said, 'Sometimes...I just get happy being with the right man,' before walking away, leaving him with 'So long, Sailor. See you on the beach sometime,' not knowing if he believed me, but feeling the weight of it myself.
grounded_facts: Charlie mentioned considering a new career like hotel management.
She contrasted her values with those of fighter pilots, emphasizing contentment in simpler things.
She expressed ambiguous hope about having found the right man.
She ended the conversation with a farewell referencing 'the beach' and left the bar.
Her departure drew attention from other men in the bar.

memories.scene_title: 145 EXT. MIRAMAR RUNWAY - DAY - MAVERICK'S JET -PULLING UP
memories.memory_text: I stood there with my hands on my hips, watching him fumble for words after he recognized me—couldn't resist needling him about how long he thought he'd stay in charge.
grounded_facts: Charlie confronts Maverick upon his return, using a teasing tone to challenge his authority.
She references his achievements—F-14, Top Gun, MiGs—before questioning what he'll do next.

memories.scene_title: 145 EXT. MIRAMAR RUNWAY - DAY - MAVERICK'S JET -PULLING UP
memories.memory_text: I pulled away at first when he put his arms around me—felt exposed on the flight line—but then I stopped caring and kissed him anyway, letting go of who might be watching.
grounded_facts: Charlie initially resists physical intimacy in the public setting of the flight line.
She overcomes her hesitation, reciprocates by putting her arms around Maverick, and kisses him without concern for onlookers.

memories.memory_text: I heard Strike call out an unknown contact inbound, and then I saw it—20 left at 25 miles, closing fast at 900 knots. Called it out clean and loud, just like protocol.
grounded_facts: Goose is the Radar Intercept Officer in the backseat.
He hears a UHF transmission from Strike reporting an unknown contact.
He detects a bogey at 20 degrees left, 25 miles away, closing at 900 knots.
He verbally reports the contact immediately after detection.

memories.memory_text: I called out the bogey—fast closure, 900 knots, 15 miles out—radar painted it clean while that snapshot of Carole and the baby sat right there between the instruments.
grounded_facts: Goose reports a radar contact at 15 miles with 900 knots closure speed.
The radar sweep is visible on a CRT screen in the cockpit.
A photograph of a woman and a baby is positioned between the instruments.

memories.memory_text: I heard that blip-blip-blip in my headset, saw the strobe on my six, and knew we were being painted—I called out the lock, but Cougar kept doubting until the tone went solid. Then it was full panic, flying ass-end forward into a MiG’s exhaust, hoping like hell he wouldn’t pull the trigger.
grounded_facts: Goose hears a repeated blip tone in the headset indicating a radar threat.
Goose observes a six o'clock strobe, suggesting an enemy radar lock from behind.
Goose reports the threat, expresses uncertainty with 'I think he's locked on us,' then later confirms 'That's missile lock!' as the tone becomes steady.
Goose uses the phrase 'Lordy! Eyeball to Asshole. Hope nobody burps!' during the close maneuver.

memories.memory_text: I told Cougar, 'Hey, if it was easy, everybody would want to come up here and do it... Instead of just us,' then he corrected me—'You'—and I let it go, knowing he was rattled but needing to keep things steady.
grounded_facts: Goose speaks to Cougar during a night carrier landing in poor visibility.
Goose delivers a line meant to calm tension by reframing the difficulty of the situation.
Cougar interrupts and corrects Goose, specifying 'you' instead of 'us'.
Mustang communicates over radio about spin, lock, and distance.

memories.memory_text: I remember shouting over the roar and motion, telling Cougar we were level, that the instruments said we were okay, even as he screamed we were inverted and hanging in our straps.
grounded_facts: Goose experienced turbulence and physical movement in the cockpit as the wing dipped repeatedly.
Goose verbally instructed Cougar to bring the aircraft left and reassured him they were level.
Goose insisted the instruments were correct when Cougar claimed they were inverted and felt like he was hanging in his straps.
Goose told Cougar to trust him and the instrument readings during the disorientation event.

memories.memory_text: I remember snapping back over the radio—'Well, we can't stay up here either'—because Cougar panicking about being upside down wasn't helping us land.
grounded_facts: Goose responds to Cougar's voiceover stating they are upside down and can't land.
Goose's line is delivered in a filtered voiceover during a cockpit scene.
The situation involves an in-air emergency where landing is a concern.

memories.memory_text: I shouted 'No! Now we're inverted!' when Cougar flipped the plane and didn't realize we were upside down — the disorientation hit hard and fast.
grounded_facts: Goose observes the aircraft is inverted after Cougar's maneuver.
Goose verbally alerts the crew with urgency upon realizing the orientation.

memories.memory_text: I yelled at Maverick to confirm our instruments were okay because Cougar didn't trust them, and I knew we were nearly out of fuel — we had to land, not eject.
grounded_facts: Goose insists the instruments are functioning correctly during Cougar's spatial disorientation.
Goose communicates fuel urgency and opposes ejection due to weather and low fuel.

memories.memory_text: I woke up after the crash, dazed and cracked a joke about valet parking, asking if they’d park it up front with the Porsches.
grounded_facts: Goose regains consciousness after the aircraft crash on the carrier deck.
He speaks first with a disoriented but humorous remark: 'Oh, Hello... Valet parking?'
He follows up by asking the silver-suited crewman to park the plane with the Porsches.

memories.memory_text: As they pulled me out, I grabbed a crewman’s radio, waved toward the bridge, and shouted 'Lucy...Ricky...I'n Home!' like Desi Arnaz.
grounded_facts: Goose is physically extracted from the aircraft by the silver-suited crewman.
He interacts with a passing crewman, asking him to check under the hood.
He takes a radio and, while being helped away, waves toward the bridge and performs a Desi Arnaz impression with the line 'Lucy...Ricky...I'n Home!'.

memories.memory_text: I yanked Cougar’s photo from the cockpit right after the crash—smoke still rising, foam spraying everywhere—and couldn’t help but crack, 'Is it extra for the hot wax?'
grounded_facts: Goose retrieved Cougar's snapshot of his wife and kid from the cockpit after the crash.
Firefighters were spraying the aircraft with foam as Goose was being led away.
Goose made a sarcastic comment about the foam treatment sounding like a car wax service.

memories.memory_text: I told Stinger I'd talk to Cougar, then begged him to reconsider—said Cougar was the best and deserved his shot at Top Gun, but he shut me down hard.
grounded_facts: Goose offers to talk to Stinger about Cougar.
Goose argues that Cougar is the best and was chosen for Top Gun.
Stinger rejects Goose's appeal, stating Cougar removed himself due to personal reasons.

memories.memory_text: Stinger turned to Mav and said 'Now you are' going to Top Gun—my gut flipped, not sure if it was pride or dread, but I looked at Mav and started to say he's great but... and let it hang.
grounded_facts: Stinger informs Maverick and Goose that they are now the new Top Gun team.
Goose exchanges a look with Maverick after the announcement.
Goose begins to qualify Maverick's skill by saying 'Mav's a great flyer but...' without finishing.

memories.memory_text: I’m gripping the seat behind Maverick, wind tearing at me as that turbo bike screams across the desert—felt like we were riding the edge of control, faster than anything should go.
grounded_facts: Goose is a passenger on a high-speed motorcycle ride in the Mojave Desert.
Maverick is driving the motorcycle.
The motorcycle is moving at an extremely high speed, with the engine described as 'screaming' and 'roaring'.
Goose is physically holding on from the back seat.

memories.scene_title: 40A ANGLE ON MAVERICK. CHP enters the frame, the usual
memories.memory_text: I followed Maverick slowly as he stepped off the bike, standing straight while the cop sized him up—I could see it in the guy's eyes, he wasn't sure whether to write a ticket or salute.
grounded_facts: Goose follows Maverick more slowly after the traffic stop.
Maverick stands at attention and interacts with the CHP officer.
The CHP officer shows hesitation and growing respect after seeing Maverick's Navy ID.
Goose observes the exchange between Maverick and the CHP without speaking.

memories.memory_text: I saw that pilot give us a big grin after Ice's jab, so I shot one right back—letting them know we weren't intimidated, riding in with CHP and all.
grounded_facts: Goose observes a second pilot grinning at Maverick and himself after Ice's comment.
Goose responds by grinning back, directly engaging the unspoken challenge.

memories.memory_text: I lit the shot, threw my head back, and chugged the flaming drink like it was nothing—felt the heat lick my face but didn’t flinch. When I slammed the glass down still burning, Slider asked how it was, and I said it could use more jet fuel.
grounded_facts: Goose lights a match and sets a shot on fire.
Goose drinks the flaming shot in one gulp and slams the glass on the bar while it's still aflame.
Slider asks Goose how it was, and Goose replies it could use more jet fuel.

memories.memory_text: I tried to drink the flaming shot again, but the flame got too close to my face—I fumbled, burned my lip, and accidentally set my hair on fire. I yelled 'Mayday! Down in flames!' and threw a beer in Sundown’s face to put it out.
grounded_facts: Goose attempts to drink another flaming shot but fails, burning his lip and setting his hair on fire.
Goose exclaims 'Mayday! Down in flames!' during the incident.
Goose throws a beer in Sundown's face to extinguish the fire on his head.

memories.memory_text: The world flipped sideways as we rolled hard, and I couldn't help but say it—'A little high on the left, don't you think?'—because Maverick was always pushing it, and someone had to keep it real.
grounded_facts: The aircraft rolled sharply, wings became perpendicular to the ground.
Goose observed the shift in orientation and made a dry comment about their position.

memories.memory_text: Hanging upside down at 300 knots, watching the runway streak past, I asked what he called that move—and when he said 'victory roll,' I said it wasn't victory, it was more like...self immolation.
grounded_facts: The aircraft was inverted, traveling at high speed down the runway.
Goose made a sarcastic remark in response to Maverick naming the maneuver a 'victory roll'.

memories.memory_text: I remember waving and calling out to the tower, feeling that rush after beating an instructor, but then seeing all those brass in the control room and thinking—no way we’re getting away with a victory roll.
grounded_facts: Goose waved and greeted the tower personnel after the flight.
Goose expressed concern about military authority figures ('guys with gold on their shoulders') possibly being in the control room.
Goose responded to Maverick’s suggestion of a victory roll by noting the consequence of losing their plane.

memories.memory_text: I pointed to the backseat of the Tomcat and asked, 'Hey, see any controls back there?'—no way they could blame me for flying hot when I don’t even have a stick.
grounded_facts: Goose speaks from the backseat of the F-14, a position without flight controls.
He directs the question rhetorically, likely in response to Jester's glare.
The line emphasizes his non-pilot role and indirect responsibility for the flight's aggressiveness.

memories.memory_text: After calling out that I had no controls, I shouted, 'And anyway...we beat the Son of a Bitch!'—felt the rush of the win, Jester’s anger didn’t matter.
grounded_facts: Goose verbally celebrates their victory over Jester despite authority disapproval.
The exclamation follows his comment about controls and precedes him running to catch up with Maverick.
The moment reflects pride in performance and solidarity with Maverick.

memories.memory_text: I looked Ice dead in the eye and told him we won, loud enough for everyone in that locker room to hear — because we did, and I wasn’t going to let him pretend otherwise.
grounded_facts: Goose says 'We won!' in the locker room after a flight exercise.
Goose speaks directly to Ice, who initially dismisses the claim.
The statement occurs in front of other pilots and students.
Ice responds by saying 'Below the hard deck doesn't count' and questions their status as the second team.

memories.memory_text: Viper turned to me and asked if I remembered the engagement the same way Maverick did, and I said yes—by the time we could respond, we were already diving into the bogey's view.
grounded_facts: Viper directly asked Goose to confirm Maverick's account of the engagement.
Goose affirmed Maverick's version, citing their tactical position during the dive.
Goose's statement was brief and focused on timing and visibility.

memories.memory_text: I saw those two get themselves into a flat spin, fighting the stick like amateurs—horizon spinning wild, pure chaos. Popped up right in front of their canopy and told 'em straight: 'Man, you guys gooned it. Your laser butts are scattered across KANSAS.'
grounded_facts: Goose's face appears at 12,000 feet in front of the cockpit canopy during a flat spin.
Hollywood and Wolfman are losing control of the jet, tumbling with a wildly swinging horizon.
Goose delivers a sarcastic critique of their failed flight maneuver.

memories.memory_text: The simulator lurched hard, alarms screaming out of nowhere—I didn’t see it coming. I just blurted, 'What happened.'
grounded_facts: The simulator experienced a sudden failure with a 'WHUMMP' sound and activated alarms.
Goose reacted verbally with 'What happened.'
The environment included intense physical sensations like lurching and realistic visuals.

memories.memory_text: One second we’re flying clean, the next the system’s dead—I turned to Maverick and snapped, 'You broke it!'
grounded_facts: Goose directly accused Maverick after the simulator failure.
The phrase 'You broke it!' was spoken by Goose in response to the malfunction.
The failure occurred immediately after Maverick executed an extremely fast maneuver.

memories.memory_text: I told Charlie 'That's what she said last night' right after she said nobody ever flew it off the template like we did, and she just stared at me until I grinned and walked off.
grounded_facts: Goose made a joke in response to Charlie's comment about flying off the template.
Charlie stared at Goose after his joke.
Goose grinned and then moved away to join the techs.

memories.scene_title: 59E THE OTHER END OF THE BEACH - LATER
memories.memory_text: I grabbed Maverick and pulled him into the game—six bucks on the line, man, we had to play.
grounded_facts: Goose initiates the volleyball game by pulling Maverick in.
Goose states he has over six dollars bet on the game.

memories.scene_title: 59E THE OTHER END OF THE BEACH - LATER
memories.memory_text: After we won, I was yelling for another round—double or nothing, twelve bucks now, it was worth pushing for.
grounded_facts: Goose urges Maverick to play another game after winning.
Goose mentions doubling the stake to twelve dollars.

memories.memory_text: I saw Carol rush toward me through the crowd, and the second I spotted her, I couldn't help but light up—kissed her hard, wrapped her up like I hadn't seen her in years, because I hadn't.
grounded_facts: Goose sees Carol emerging from the crowd and immediately smiles.
Goose greets Carol with a kiss and embraces her.

memories.memory_text: I spotted Ricky hiding behind Carol's skirts, and just like always, I scooped him up and spun him around—his laugh cracked me open, same as it does every time after six months apart.
grounded_facts: Goose looks around for Ricky, then notices him hiding behind Carol.
Goose picks up Ricky and spins him around in a playful manner, a recurring game between them.

memories.memory_text: I slid the Tomcat up right behind and above the MiG, canopy to canopy—so close I could see the pilot’s face. I held his eyes for a second, then flipped him the bird.
grounded_facts: Maverick positions the F-14 directly above and behind the MiG with precise flying.
The canopies are nearly touching, allowing visual contact between pilots.
Maverick makes eye contact and flips off the MiG pilot.

memories.memory_text: After the flyover, Wizard called low fuel—4.0 remaining. I keyed the radio for Cougar, said I was getting hungry, told him to head for the barn. No response. I asked again, 'Where are you?'
grounded_facts: Wizard reports fuel level at 4.0, indicating urgency.
Maverick initiates disengagement by calling Cougar on the radio.
Maverick uses coded language ('getting hungry') to signal fuel state.
Maverick expresses concern about missing wingman by asking for location.

memories.memory_text: I saw Cougar's plane flip inverted in those dark turbulent clouds, and when he didn't respond, I keyed the mic and told him to half-roll it—now.
grounded_facts: Maverick observed Cougar's aircraft become inverted
Maverick issued the command 'COUGAR, THIS IS MAVERICK. HALF ROLL IT. NOW!'
The command was given after Cougar failed to respond to initial warnings

memories.memory_text: I stayed on Cougar's wing and told him he was on the ball after he rolled upright.
grounded_facts: Maverick flew wingtip-to-wingtip with Cougar's aircraft after the half-roll
Maverick said 'COUGAR, YOU'RE ON THE BALL' to reassure him
Cougar experienced vertigo and believed he was still inverted despite being level

memories.memory_text: I saluted the CHP lieutenant after he told me he was Top Gun outside the gate, and he gave me a sharp salute back—respect earned, even if it was just for a moment.
grounded_facts: Maverick says 'Yes sir!' after the CHP's statement
Maverick salutes the CHP
The CHP returns a snappy salute

memories.memory_text: Two pilots in flight suits watched me pass—Ice made a comment about the police escort, and I turned my head slightly, staring over my shades at them, saying nothing but making it clear I wasn't intimidated.
grounded_facts: Ice and Hollywood stare at Maverick as he passes through the gate
Ice says, 'Uh oh, police escort. This one must be a real killer.'
Maverick turns slightly and stares over his shades at them
Goose grins back at the pilots

memories.memory_text: I walked into the O-Club with Goose, Hollywood, and Wolfman—music pounding, cars lined up outside—felt like stepping into the fight, but on the ground.
grounded_facts: Maverick arrives at the O-Club at night with Goose, Hollywood, and Wolfman.
It is Wednesday, designated as 'Animal Night' at the club.
There is loud music and fast cars present, creating a charged, energetic atmosphere.

memories.scene_title: 47A WOXOF BAR. She returns, spots the Captain, motioning to
memories.memory_text: She touched my collar and adjusted my insignia. She smiled and asked where my wingman was. I said nobody. She said too bad and walked away.
grounded_facts: Charlie adjusted Maverick's insignia
Charlie smiled
Charlie asked where Maverick's wingman was
Maverick responded that nobody was watching his six
Charlie said 'Too bad' and left

memories.scene_title: 47A WOXOF BAR. She returns, spots the Captain, motioning to
memories.memory_text: I asked if I could walk her out, and for a second she turned back with a smile—then said she was with someone, just like that.
grounded_facts: Maverick initiated the question 'Can I walk you out?'
Charlie initially responded by turning back with a smile
Charlie stated she was with someone

memories.memory_text: I waited in the back seat until she started driving, then sat up—her sharp yelp when she saw me, the way she jerked the wheel, the sudden brightness in her eyes in the rearview. I’d startled her good.
grounded_facts: Maverick was hiding in the back seat of Charlie's car.
He revealed himself after she started driving.
Charlie was startled and nearly lost control of the car.

memories.memory_text: After she asked how I knew it was her car, I gave her the line about fast and pretty things matching her—then said it straight: 'I think I'm in love.' My voice didn’t waver, even though the moment felt too big to fit in the car.
grounded_facts: Maverick explained how he identified Charlie's car with a mix of flattery and humor.
He followed with the statement 'I think I'm in love.'
The declaration was direct and delivered without hesitation in tone.

memories.memory_text: I confronted her in the hall, asked why she didn’t tell me she was a TAGREF—she just looked at me like I was some rookie who didn’t know the game. Said I was determined to make a fool of myself anyway.
grounded_facts: Maverick directly asked Charlie why she didn't reveal she was a TAGREF.
Charlie responded coolly, implying Maverick's behavior was self-inflicted.
Maverick felt exposed and embarrassed in front of peers who were watching and grinning.

memories.memory_text: After she walked away, I saw Viper leaning in the doorway, pipe in hand, asking me about my call sign like he was testing whether I deserved it. I told him it ran in the family.
grounded_facts: Viper approached Maverick after the encounter with Charlie.
Viper questioned the origin of Maverick's call sign in a detached, probing manner.
Maverick responded briefly and formally, sensing an unspoken evaluation.

memories.memory_text: I beat an instructor in a dogfight and said we should do a victory roll—Goose was worried, but I didn’t care. We broke over the runway, rolling out high and fast.
grounded_facts: Maverick states they beat an instructor in a dogfight.
Maverick suggests doing a victory roll as a celebration.
Goose expresses concern about consequences.
The aircraft rolls out and breaks over the runway.

memories.memory_text: I walked in with Goose, helmet in hand, and the room lit up—fists in the air, guys yelling, Wolfman asking if we won. I said, 'Didn't everybody?' and they all laughed. We were up, we were flying, we beat them clean.
grounded_facts: Maverick enters the locker room with Goose after a flight exercise.
Other pilots react with celebration and admiration toward Maverick and Goose.
Wolfman asks 'You won?!' and Maverick responds with 'Didn't everybody?'
The atmosphere is initially celebratory and affirming for Maverick.

memories.memory_text: Ice walked in, got his usual hero's welcome, then turned and said, 'Below the hard deck doesn't count.' I started to step toward him—he didn't even look at me when he called us the second team. Like we didn't matter.
grounded_facts: Iceman enters the locker room after Maverick and receives celebratory attention.
Iceman initially speaks into his locker, saying 'That's not what I heard.'
After Goose declares 'We won!', Iceman responds, 'Below the hard deck doesn't count. You guys are the second team, aren't you?'
Maverick begins to approach Iceman before being interrupted by Jester.

memories.memory_text: I stood there silent at first, then told Viper I had him in view when I broke the hard deck—I was peeling over the egg into a dive, and there wasn't any danger. I could feel the weight of the day, the jet, the call, but I kept my voice low and steady.
grounded_facts: Maverick was questioned by Viper about breaking the rules of engagement during a flyby.
He explains he continued the engagement past the hard deck because he had the target in sight and perceived no danger.
Maverick initially gives no response, then speaks in a quiet, exhausted voice.
He later acknowledges the rule violation with a controlled, slightly over-sincere admission of over-enthusiasm.

memories.memory_text: I was on the MiG's tail, pulled into a hard turn, and everything started screaming—then the alarms hit and the system collapsed.
grounded_facts: Maverick was flying aggressively in a simulation and got on the tail of a MiG.
He executed a fast, tight maneuver that immediately preceded the system failure.
The cockpit experienced a sudden loss of normal function with alarms and lights activating.

memories.memory_text: I told her I was sorry for being out of line about the MiG — I meant it, and she just said 'apology acknowledged,' like it was official business.
grounded_facts: Maverick apologizes to Charlie for his earlier behavior regarding the MiG comment.
Charlie responds formally with 'apology acknowledged,' indicating professional distance.

memories.memory_text: I asked her out even after she said no, kept pushing — told her I don't give up, and when I left, I said 'Let's make it at eight,' and she didn't say no again.
grounded_facts: Maverick persists in asking Charlie out despite her initial rejection and emphasis on professionalism.
He asserts his determination by stating he doesn't give up and proposes a time for a date, which Charlie does not explicitly reject before closing the door.

memories.memory_text: I had the shot. It dies. We live. That’s just how it is—clean, direct, no pretending.
grounded_facts: Maverick killed a fish and stated 'I had the shot' when questioned.
He followed with 'It dies. We live,' framing the act as a necessary exchange.
He believes killing is more honest when done directly and acknowledged.

memories.memory_text: She didn’t like seeing the fish struggle. Said I was horrible. But she eats meatballs—someone still had to kill for that.
grounded_facts: Charlie reacted negatively to the fish's death throes and called Maverick horrible.
Maverick pointed out the hypocrisy in rejecting direct killing while consuming food that also required death.
He emphasized awareness of death in everyday acts like breathing and eating.

memories.memory_text: I had Viper in the diamond, solid tone—just about to fire—when Goose called out two bogies, three and nine o'clock high. They came out of nowhere, and I froze for a second, realizing I'd flown straight into a trap.
grounded_facts: Maverick had Viper in the targeting diamond with a strong missile tone.
Goose alerted to two incoming bogies at three and nine o'clock high.
The ambush came suddenly, catching Maverick off guard.
Maverick's eyes showed surprise and shock in the moment.

memories.memory_text: After the kill was called, I heard Viper say 'WALKED RIGHT INTO IT,' and Jester added that Zorro got my wingman. Then Goose mocked me, slapping his helmet—sounded like the worst kind of truth wrapped in a joke.
grounded_facts: Viper taunted, 'WALKED RIGHT INTO IT,' after the engagement.
Jester stated, 'ZORRO GOT YOUR WINGMAN. NICE GOING.'
Goose delivered a sarcastic message about dead sons and stupidity, then slapped his helmet.
The tone of the cockpit shifted to ridicule and reprimand.

memories.memory_text: Viper told me I fly reckless—great instincts, no discipline—and that talent isn't a holy shield. He said following emotions instead of my wingman was a stupid mistake, one that gets people killed.
grounded_facts: Viper criticized Maverick's flying as reckless and undisciplined
Viper emphasized that talent alone does not protect a pilot
Viper stated that Maverick's decision to follow emotions over wingman protocol was a cardinal sin

memories.memory_text: Before he left, Viper touched my shoulder—just a quick press—and something in me let go. I don't know why, but that small gesture made the weight ease, like I was still part of the tribe.
grounded_facts: Viper touched Maverick's shoulder as he walked away
The touch caused Maverick to visibly relax from prior tension
The moment occurred after Viper's critique and final remarks

memories.memory_text: Food...and you...my F-14. Then I saw her smile and knew she was teasing—so I said it again, backwards. She laughed. I told her about night landings, how it’s the greatest high, living at one hundred and ten percent.
grounded_facts: Maverick says 'Food...and you...my F-14!' after surfacing from the pool.
Charlie questions the order, and Maverick replies 'Well no...inverse order.'
Maverick describes night carrier landings as 'the most dangerous thing you can think of' and 'the greatest high in the world.'

memories.memory_text: My flight suit slipped off the chair. I didn’t see it, but she picked it up—and then that thing fell out. She held it up, quiet. I grabbed it fast, shoved it back in my pocket. Said it was just a lucky charm. She didn’t believe me. I told her pawn shop. Changed the subject.
grounded_facts: Maverick’s flight suit falls to the ground.
A Navy Cross falls from the pocket when Charlie picks up the suit.
Maverick quickly takes the Navy Cross from Charlie and puts it back in his pocket.
He claims it’s a lucky charm and says he got it at a pawn shop when asked.

memories.scene_title: 85A BOOM - A HURRICANE OF WIND AND NOISE
memories.memory_text: I told Goose to eject, and the next thing I saw was him slamming into the canopy—there was a sickening crunch before I got blown out of the jet.
grounded_facts: Maverick said 'Let's go. Eject.'
Goose pulled the ejection handle and was launched upward
Goose collided with the spinning canopy immediately after ejection
The collision produced a 'sickening crunch' sound
Maverick was ejected after Goose, just before the plane spun in

memories.memory_text: I pulled on the parachute cords, fighting the weight under the water until I saw Goose’s body surface—he was limp, already gone.
grounded_facts: Maverick physically pulled a submerged body to the surface using parachute cords
The body retrieved was identified as Goose
Goose was confirmed dead upon surfacing

memories.memory_text: I dragged Goose into the raft and held him in my arms, the dye spreading in the water around us, but I couldn’t think—my mind just shut down.
grounded_facts: Maverick brought Goose's body into the raft
He physically held Goose's body
He experienced a mental shutdown after the act

memories.memory_text: I held onto Goose as the frogman tried to pull him away, telling me to let go, but I couldn't—I wouldn't—until I finally did, and then I just watched him float lifeless in the water as they lifted me up.
grounded_facts: Maverick physically resists the frogman's attempt to take Goose's body.
Maverick releases Goose only after a prolonged moment of resistance.
Maverick observes Goose's body in the water from his POV as he is hoisted by the helicopter.
Maverick shivers from the cold during the rescue.

memories.memory_text: I was flying that jet—it was my responsibility. I keep thinking maybe I shouldn’t even take the flight pay, not after what happened to Goose.
grounded_facts: Maverick states he was flying and felt responsible for Goose's death.
Maverick questions whether he should continue receiving flight pay.
The conversation occurs at night, in Charlie's sports car, with 'Stand by Me' playing on the radio.

memories.scene_title: 88A ON THE BEACH - NIGHT AND FOG
memories.memory_text: I stood at the edge of the surf, bare feet in the sand, and said I wanted it back—yesterday. I didn’t care how impossible it sounded.
grounded_facts: Maverick says 'I want it back' and specifies 'Yesterday' in response to Charlie's question.
Maverick kicks the sand with his bare feet while near the surf.

memories.scene_title: 88A ON THE BEACH - NIGHT AND FOG
memories.memory_text: I told her that if we knew then what we know now, we might never have come up out of the sea—I said it like I meant it, but I wasn’t sure I believed it.
grounded_facts: Maverick says: 'If we knew then, what we know now, we might never have come up out of there.'
Charlie responds by saying 'You don't believe that,' indicating Maverick's statement carried uncertainty.

memories.scene_title: 89C INT. CONFERENCE ROOM - DAY - MAVERICK'S POV
memories.memory_text: I stood there in dress whites while the Commander read the verdict—cleared of fault, flight status restored—but I didn’t say a word. I couldn’t feel the relief they expected, not with Goose still gone.
grounded_facts: Maverick is present in the conference room wearing dress whites.
The Board of Inquiry declares Maverick not at fault in the accident.
Maverick's record is cleared and he is restored to flight status.
Maverick does not respond verbally or visibly to the announcement.

memories.memory_text: I threw my gear into the bag and kept moving—Fungus tried to say something, but there was nothing left to say. When he asked what I was doing, I told him I was saving them some paperwork.
grounded_facts: Maverick is packing his belongings from his locker.
Fungus enters and questions Maverick's actions.
Maverick responds with 'Saving them some paperwork.'
Maverick does not stop or engage further with Fungus.

memories.memory_text: I looked at Ice, saw him give me a thumbs up, and keyed the mic—'Boss, you better clear it out, we're five minutes out and we're doing it!'
grounded_facts: Maverick observes Ice giving a thumbs up.
Maverick communicates with Boss, declaring they are five minutes out and proceeding with the flyby.
